_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Toxins and Environmental: Hyperthermia/Heat Exposure. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Hyperthermia/Heat Exposure

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Hyperthermia/Heat Exposure
points: Cooling and rehydration
Mitigate high-risk for decompensation
Mitigate high-risk for agitation and uncooperative behavior

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Heat cramps
Heat exhaustion
Heat stroke
Heat syncope
Heat edema
Stimulant drug abuse
Delirium with agitated behavior [See Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency Guideline]
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Fever from infectious or inflammatory conditions
Malignant hyperthermia
Serotonin syndrome
Neuroleptic malignant syndrome

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Move patient to cool environment; remove excess clothing
Aggressive cooling: cool water misting + fanning (preferred); ice packs to axilla, groin, neck
Oxygen via NRM; BVM for respiratory failure
CPR/AED for cardiac arrest (do NOT defer CPR because of high temperature)
Oral water or electrolyte drink for mild heat exhaustion (alert patient only)

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Aggressive cooling: ice immersion (bath/sheet) for heat stroke (temp >40°C with AMS) — most effective method
IV access maintenance (non-medicated); cool IV fluids preferred
12-lead ECG: dysrhythmias common with severe hyperthermia
Blood glucose monitoring
Supraglottic airway for airway compromise in heat stroke coma

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access: cool NS 1-2 L IV for hypotension in heat exhaustion (caution with heat stroke + myocardial dysfunction)
Blood glucose monitoring and correction
Waveform capnography for monitoring
Benzodiazeines (midazolam IV) for heat stroke with seizures

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: Cold IV saline 1-2 L at 4°C (if available) — internal cooling method
Target core temperature: cool aggressively to ≤39°C; stop active cooling to prevent overshoot hypothermia
Benzodiazepines for shivering or seizures: lorazepam 2-4 mg IV; avoid phenothiazines (impair thermoregulation)
RSI and intubation for heat stroke coma or seizures with airway compromise
Vasopressors (norepinephrine) for refractory hypotension unresponsive to cooling and fluids
12-lead ECG interpretation: treat dysrhythmias per specific protocol
Monitor for rhabdomyolysis, DIC, hepatic failure, renal failure — fluid resuscitation and hospital notification

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Consider use of physical securing devices [See Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency Guideline] to protect vascular access sites.

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Patients at risk for heat emergencies include neonates, infants, geriatric patients, and patients with mental illness
Contributory risk factors may come from: a. Prescription and over-the-counter herbal supplements

Heat exposure can occur either due to increased environmental temperatures or prolonged exercise or a combination of both a. Environments with temperature greater than 90'F and humidity greater than 60% present the most risk
Heat stroke is associated with cardiac arrhythmias independent of drug ingestion/overdose Heat stroke has also been associated with cerebral edema
For patients with signs and symptoms of heat stroke, rapid cooling takes priority over other interventions (e.g., cardiac monitoring, IV access)

Do not forget to look for other causes of altered mental status such as low blood glucose level, or, in the proper circumstances (i.e., endurance exercise events), consider exercise associated hyponatremia (EAH), especially in the patient with altered mental status, normal blood glucose, and normal temperature
Controversy: shivering may occur while treating heat stroke a. It is uncertain how harmful shivering is to heat stroke patients
Hyperthermia not from environmental factors has a differential that includes the following: a. Fever and delirium

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Patient assessment includes all types of medication/drug use and detailed past medical history
' Environmental assessment performed
' Cooling interventions considered and implemented
' Decision-making regarding securing devices
' Decision-making regarding monitoring ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation)

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Consider use of physical securing devices [See Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency Guideline] to protect vascular access sites.
Delirium with agitated behavior [See Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency Guideline]
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
